operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+6-float Generic Truth Value (14f2b977ffa98d75)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: 6-float Generic Truth Value
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-08-14 to 2015-08-15
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f2b977ffa98d75

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Hendy Irawan
Date: 2015-08-14

Okay, now I map it as either: no STV: 0-floats, or STV: 3-floats [strength, confidence, count]. If forced to 6-floats, then (stv 0.7 0.3) becomes [0.7, 0.3, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0]. Is 'missing TV' same as (stv 0.0 0.0)?

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-14

A missing count is the same as a zero count. All zeros for a missing TV sounds fine.

--linas

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Hendy Irawan
Date: 2015-08-15

Thank you Linas for confirmation. That's a relief :)

Hendy

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+about indentation, tab or space (14abf409f90998de)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: about indentation, tab or space
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-01-06 to 2015-01-08
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14abf409f90998de

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-01-06

I'm resurrecting this old thread after noticing the smart-tab indentation in some of Linas's code. I find the smart-tab indentation pretty neat. Rather than trying to enforce Linas to use space indentation, what about accepting a double standard, space and smart-tab?

In the absence of feedback within 48h I'll update the wiki with the double standard rule.

Nil

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-01-08

I've updated the wiki to indicate that smart-tab is tolerated http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Development_standards#Whitespace

if that turns out to be a bad idea we can add more restriction later on.

Nil

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-01-08

I've also added links to vim and emacs plugins
http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Development_standards#Vim
http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Development_standards#Emacs

Nil

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+agglomerative clustering in OpenCog via the URE (15152284cdc7ac3e)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: agglomerative clustering in OpenCog via the URE
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-11-29 to 2015-12-04
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 15152284cdc7ac3e

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-29

Dawit, Here is a summary of my thoughts about doing Omniclust-style agglomerative clustering using the OpenCog Rule Engine. We can implement agglomerative clustering using the URE, via implementing inference rules for merging Atoms into sets, merging Atoms into existing sets, and merging two sets. For meaningful clustering, careful attention to premise selection heuristics would be needed.

-- Ben

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-04

FYI I put the agglomerative clustering idea on the wiki site: http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/Agglomerative_Clustering_in_Atomspace_using_the_URE

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+ANNOUNCE Link Grammar version 5.2.0 is now available. (14a8d6f21473b6df)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: ANNOUNCE Link Grammar version 5.2.0 is now available.
Messages: 3
Date range: 2014-12-27 to 2015-01-03
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14a8d6f21473b6df

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2014-12-27

Link Grammar version 5.2.0 is now available. Download from:



http://www.abisource.com/downloads/link-grammar/5.2.0/link-grammar-5.2.0.tar.gz



This is a major release of the parser, with many important changes in

it.  The internals of the parser have been re-organized, resulting in

a speedup of 2x to 4x for typical English texts.  Multiple multi-

threading bugs were fixed, and there is now a simple multi-threading

unit test.  A memory leak was fixed, and a memory over-consumption

bug was fixed.  These changes were enabled by the final removal of the

"fat link" code from the parser.



Parser internals work continues apace: it is expected that a version

5.3.0 will follow shortly, featuring a completely re-designed tokenizer.

This redesign should enable simpler and better morpholo

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Brett Ward
Date: 1 Jan 2015

Hi All,



Wonder if someone can give me some noob help...

I'm setting back up after a long break. New install of Trusty 14.04.

Most of opencog tests pass.



Now I'm trying to compile link-grammar, and there is something I'm missing. 

I've tried using http://wiki.opencog. [...]

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-01-03

Hi Brett,



I just release version 5.2.2, which I believe will fix this problem.



here: http://www.abisource.com/downloads/link-grammar/current/



-- Linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+ANNOUNCE Link Grammar version 5.3.0 is now available (1512ff1722cc658d)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: ANNOUNCE Link Grammar version 5.3.0 is now available
Messages: 4
Date range: 2015-11-22 to 2015-12-04
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 1512ff1722cc658d

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-22

Link Grammar version 5.3.0 is now available. This is a major release. Most fundamentally, the tokenizer has been completely redesigned. The python bindings are completely redesigned. Various fixes: the SAT solver is no longer crippled. Performance speedups especially for longer sentences.

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-22

It never goes without a hitch. Lets try again with version 5.3.1, which actually compiles!

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-12-03

Amir created the new tokenizer. Rather than just creating a single tokenization, it creates multiple different alternatives. Our favorite example is seasondbeach which could be tokenized as sea sand beach, seas and beach, or seasoned beech. The data structure is best envisioned as a collection of forking paths that split and rejoin. The long-term goal is to assign different weights to the different paths.

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: amirpli@gmail.com
Date: 2015-12-04

The tokenizer uses a graph whose nodes are subwords. Initially sentence words are tokenized by whitespace. Then each subword gets split further if needed. The best way to understand the word-graph is to watch it using the graphviz display feature. [...]

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Article MIT claims to have found a language universal (14f12e83b3e1be70)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Article MIT claims to have found a language universal
Messages: 6
Date range: 2015-08-09 to 2015-08-13
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f12e83b3e1be70

============================================================

--- Message 1/6 ---
From: Terry Woodward
Date: 2015-08-09

MIT article with DLM concept may be of interest.

--- Message 2/6 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-08-09

The result is nice but very unsurprising and certainly provides no evidence for a Chomsky-style principles and parameters approach to linguistics. The universal they propose would seem a natural consequence of any language designed for use by agents whose working memories have some sort of rapid decay function.

--- Message 3/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-09

The idea of dependency-length minimization is old -- dating back at least 30 years, and was one of the ideas that influenced the development of link-grammar. Other measures proposed include graph centrality and hubiness, with arguments that hubiness is a more accurate measure.

--- Message 4/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-12

Hubiness may be a better model of sentence complexity than mean dependency distance. Extensive bibliography on dependency-length minimization: idea traced back to Yngve 1960. Key refs include Temperley 2008, Park and Levy 2009, Gibson 2000, Haitao Liu 2008, Hawkins 1994 and 2004.

--- Message 5/6 ---
From: Richard Hudson
Date: 2015-08-13

Surprised if hubiness measures processing difficulty better than dependency length because degree without mention of order does not distinguish DHD from DDH. Dependency length too crude, needs sophistication.

--- Message 6/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-13

Multiple issues make this hard to sort out. Even if a formula is found, the original claim of psychological plausibility means lab experiments still have to be designed and run. This seems like a good-sized research program, not something to be quickly knocked off.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Atom properties (150a35a73cdf20c0)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Atom properties
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-10-26 to 2015-10-26
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150a35a73cdf20c0

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-26

Everyone and Ben,

There is a natural way to use the StateLink to set 'atom properties'. The StateLink is a way to store key-value pairs in the atomspace, such that there can only ever be one 'value' for a given 'key'. To store a number, use the NumberNode.

At this time, this is not terribly efficient; I'm working on that. It does work, though, and its how I store tunable parameters for the behavior tree.

-- Linas

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Jim Rutt
Date: 2015-10-26

Useful. It's the silent deleting of old values that is most obviously new here.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+automated discovery of grammatical classes (14e25d53c74ad79b)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: automated discovery of grammatical classes
Messages: 4
Date range: 2015-06-24 to 2015-06-30
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14e25d53c74ad79b

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-24

See the attached PDF, it explains some of the ideas more clearly. The very next goal is to explore the different similarity metrics on your database of real data, and see how well the different metrics might do. This will require writing a bunch of code and generating more lists, more graphs.

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-25

The attached includes formulas for the frequency and the MI of disjuncts. In addition to the data collection and analysis, new code is needed to perform the actual groupings of words into classes, and that the merge discovery process has to be able to merge words into existing classes.

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-26

A period denotes the end of a speech act, and it is a valid part of language. It has a real semantic meaning. Just like some words only occur at the beginning of sentences, other words occur only at the end.

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-30

A vector space is a set of objects with a single floating point number associated with each element of the set. The set of objects is called the basis. Each disjunct is a basis vector. A word-vector is constructed by listing how many times each disjunct occurs with that word.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+A certain sentence parse gives a 'wrong type argument' exception (150db3f887b2ee88)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: A certain sentence parse gives a 'wrong type argument' exception
Messages: 3
Date range: 5 Nov 2015 to 2015-11-06
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150db3f887b2ee88

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Tareq Alkhaldi
Date: 5 Nov 2015

Hi all, So I loaded the r2l rulebase then tried to parse: (nlp-parse "An Italian became the world's greatest tenor") But I got an error: In procedure cog-new-link: Wrong type argument in position 2 (expecting opencog atom): #<unspecified>

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: William Ma
Date: 5 Nov 2015

Hummm... on RelEx it seems the _det relation is triggered incorrectly. As far as I know _det should only be triggered for "those", "these", "this", "that".

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-06

In relex _det identifies any determiner, the most common of which are a, an, the. These and those are less common.

The stack trace is almost impossible to decode: C++ was expecting an atom, but didn't get it. Perhaps some of the r2l rules could be designed to throw exceptions (with easy-to-understand error messages) when the wrong format is encountered?

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+A New Contributor - Helping with the Python Interfaces (14b800286c7a6e41)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: A New Contributor - Helping with the Python Interfaces
Messages: 8
Date range: 2015-02-12 to 2015-02-15
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14b800286c7a6e41

============================================================

--- Message 1/8 ---
From: Curtis M. Faith
Date: 2015-02-12

I am interested in helping improve the Python interfaces to Opencog. Ive got the Cogserver running in docker on my Mac. Im a longtime programmer since the early 80s. C++ is my bread and butter language. What is the best way for me to work with the existing team?

--- Message 2/8 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-02-12

We have an IRC channel but its rather inactive: #opencog on freenode. This list is the best place to discuss. One way to get started is to pick one of the python bugs on github and fix it. Probably the easiest one would be issue #1245.

--- Message 3/8 ---
From: Richard Emslie
Date: 2015-02-12

Id also be interested in helping improve the Python interfaces. I met the Hong Kong team during the New Year break. My thoughts were to use CFFI for binding to the atomspace.

--- Message 4/8 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-02-14

Welcome to the project! The mailing list is the best place for discussion. After modifying existing code, run the unit tests. When a change is ready, submit a pull request on GitHub.

--- Message 5/8 ---
From: Curtis M. Faith
Date: 2015-02-14

Thank you for your replies. I look forward to contributing.

--- Message 6/8 ---
From: Curtis M. Faith
Date: 2015-02-15

My default approach is to NOT consider changing tools unless they are clearly not up to the task. Cython does the intended job well. The project already uses Cython so were not starting from scratch.

--- Message 7/8 ---
From: Curtis M. Faith
Date: 2015-02-15

Currently getting seven unit test failures on docker image. Ill dig into the PythonModuleTests failure.

--- Message 8/8 ---
From: Cosmo Harrigan
Date: 2015-02-15

Yes, that makes sense. If your changes are related to the Cython code, AllPythonModuleTests is probably not relevant. In general, submit small pull requests for small changes.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+A question about 'make test' target (150db9d988506fcc)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: A question about 'make test' target
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-11-06 to 2015-11-06
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150db9d988506fcc

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Hedayat Vatankhah
Date: 2015-11-06

Dear all, Hi! I'm a new member here. There is an ambiguity about the 'make test' target. CMake also considers it a standard target, but by default creates this target itself when ENABLE_TESTING() is used. I'd like to know if test target is intentionally overridden.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-06

I don't know about cmake, but `make test` is a standard target that almost all packages provide. For gnu autoconf targets, its usually called `make check`.

--linas

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Hedayat Vatankhah
Date: 2015-11-06

Maybe I didn't explain well. CMake also considers it a standard target, but by default it creates this target itself when ENABLE_TESTING() is used so it doesn't expect us to define this target manually. What I'd like to know is if there is any problems with CMake's own test support.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Backward Chaining Rules vs random ImplicationLink (14c786bb0409d953)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Backward Chaining Rules vs random ImplicationLink
Messages: 4
Date range: 1 Apr 2015 to 2015-04-02
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14c786bb0409d953

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: William Ma
Date: 1 Apr 2015

In trying to improve the BackwardChainer, I came across a problem: There's the concept of Rules (defined as BindLink with ImplicationLink), and then there's also random ImplicationLink in the AtomSpace. How to backward chain on the ImplicationLink, when it itself is not a Rule?

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-04-02

I think the fundamental problem is that the pattern matching rules should not be represented as ImplicationLinks, cause it's not coherent with PLN semantics.

I think instead they should be split in 2 parts: P, a predicate for finding matches; T, a schema for turning those matches into atoms.

Nil

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-04-02

Hmmm... I don't understand the problem (yet)...

Case (1) has no unbound variables AND has a high truth value, so there's really no point to backward chaining on it. On the other hand, case (2) has two unbound variables so there is a point to backward chaining on it.

-- Ben

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-04-02

Linas and Nil and I have recently realized it's confusing. So we plan to make careful sense of it all after the Easter break...

Nil has argued to get rid of implicit binding and I suspect he's right --- implicit binding seems to cause too much confusion. In that case every variable would need to be bound by some LambdaLink.

-- Ben
